turns.content: Hey, I'm working on a weekend side project and I have this array of integers. I tried using sort to order it and then fill with the first element to propagate values, but it feels clunky. Is there a cleaner way to handle this propagation without multiple passes?

Hey! You can definitely simplify that by using the rotate_left method combined with binary_search. If you want to shift elements cyclically, rotate_left is perfect for moving data positions in a single step. For finding where a value should go based on indices, binary_search gives you the exact position without scanning linearly.

Oh, I see what you're saying with rotate_left. But wait, if I'm just trying to fill every position from index 0 onwards with a specific value, does binary_search even apply here? It seems like fill is the right call, so why am I overthinking this?

You're absolutely right about fill being the direct approach for that use case. The confusion comes from mixing up rotation logic with filling logic. If you want to replace a range starting at index 0, just call fill on that slice directly rather than trying to combine binary_search or rotate_left unnecessarily.

I've been reading about slice methods like sort and fill but I still don't get how they actually interact with the data. I tried to use binary_search on a vector of strings but it failed because the values weren't in order, and now I'm stuck trying to figure out why my code won't compile when I call rotate_left or windows without sorting first.

The issue is that many slice methods like binary_search require the data to be pre-sorted according to a specific ordering, so you can't just apply them to an unsorted vector. When you call rotate_left or windows on a mixed-up collection, those operations don't behave as expected because they assume a predictable sequence. You need to sort your data using the appropriate comparator before calling binary_search or any other method that depends on order. Think of it like this: if you try to find an item in a messy pile without organizing it first, the search will fail no matter how good your algorithm is.
